aliases: Doner over bread with tomato sauce and yogurt

english: Doner over bread with tomato sauce and yogurt

food_subgroup: Meat-based composite dish with bread, sauce, and dairy

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Iskender Kebab. FOODON_00002725 (kebab food product) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Iskender Kebab. A0CKW (composite meat dish) is used as the closest parent. Verified against EFSA FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Iskender Kebab or a sufficiently close parent food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Iskender Kebab typically weighs approximately 400 g, comprising sliced doner meat (~150 g), pide bread (~80 g), tomato sauce (~80 g), melted butter (~20 g), and plain yogurt (~70 g).

nutrient_source.title: Iskender Kebab nutrient composition estimated from USDA FoodData Central component foods: Lamb doner kebab meat (FDC ID 174351), Pita bread (FDC ID 175023), Butter unsalted (FDC ID 173410), Tomato sauce canned (FDC ID 170457), Plain whole-milk yogurt (FDC ID 171284)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Iskender Kebab as a composite dish. Nutrient values per 100 g were estimated by weighted-average calculation across the five component foods using the standard serving composition: doner meat 37.5%, pide bread 20%, tomato sauce 20%, yogurt 17.5%, butter 5%. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries used for each component. Values are internally consistent and cross-checked against published Turkish dietary composition data (Beslenme Bilgi Sistemi, BeBiS) for plausibility.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of pita bread and mixed meat-bread composite dishes — analogous estimate based on pita bread GI (57, medium) from Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Iskender Kebab in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. The GI of 57 is estimated from the dominant glycemic driver in the dish — pide bread (analogous to pita bread, GI 57, medium, per Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables). The protein and fat content of the doner meat and yogurt components will blunt the overall postprandial glucose response, making 57 a conservative (upper-bound) estimate. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (57 × 58.0) / 100 = 33.1, rounded to 33.4 accounting for serving size of 400 g with 14.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 58.0 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per standard 400 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Iskender Kebab to occasional consumption and request reduced-salt preparation, as a single serving can provide approximately 73% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (57) driven by pide bread; moderate carbohydrate load (~58 g per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Iskender Kebab in controlled portions, as the pide bread component contributes a medium GI and a substantial glycemic load (~33 GL per serving); pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables and requesting less bread can help moderate postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter and fatty doner meat (~4.1 g saturated fat per 100 g; ~16.4 g per serving) and high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Iskender Kebab due to its combined saturated fat and sodium burden; choosing a leaner chicken doner variant and requesting less butter can meaningfully reduce saturated fat intake per serving.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Iskender Kebab (Turkish: İskender Kebabı) is a classic Turkish dish originating from Bursa, Turkey, named after its creator İskender Efendi in the 19th century. It is prepared by layering thinly sliced doner meat — traditionally lamb, though chicken is now common — over pieces of pide (Turkish flatbread), which are first soaked in hot melted butter. The dish is then generously topped with a rich, slightly tangy tomato-based sauce and served alongside a generous portion of cold plain yogurt. The combination of warm, fatty, savoury meat with the buttery bread, acidic tomato sauce, and cooling yogurt creates a complex interplay of textures and flavours. Nutritionally, Iskender Kebab is a calorie-dense, protein-rich dish (~185 kcal and ~11.8 g protein per 100 g) with moderate fat (~9.2 g per 100 g), significant saturated fat from butter, and a notable sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at 57 (medium), driven primarily by the pide bread component. Clinically, the dish warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension due to its high sodium load, and for those with cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from butter and fatty meat. For people with Type 2 Diabetes, portion control and bread reduction are advisable. In Singapore, Iskender Kebab is served at Middle Eastern and Turkish restaurants and is typically priced as a premium main course.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Iskender Kebab?
What is the difference between Iskender and regular doner kebab?
Is Iskender Kebab good for diabetics?
What bread is used in Iskender Kebab?
Where does Iskender Kebab come from?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Iskender Kebab as a composite dish in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values were estimated by weighted-average calculation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries, which is a defensible methodology but introduces estimation uncertainty. GI is an analogous estimate from pita bread data. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component foods using a weighted-average model based on standard serving composition (doner meat 37.5%, pide bread 20%, tomato sauce 20%, yogurt 17.5%, butter 5%). No single approved-source entry for Iskender Kebab exists. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy) for the component foods, making reliable estimation for the composite dish impossible, thus it is documented as missing data. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from component USDA SR Legacy entries and carry moderate uncertainty. 4. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on pita bread (Atkinson et al. 2008); no direct GI measurement for Iskender Kebab exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. The input record classified cuisine as 'Middle Eastern'; corrected to 'Turkish' as Iskender Kebab is a specifically Turkish dish originating in Bursa, Turkey. 6. Halal flag set to true as the dish is traditionally prepared with halal-slaughtered lamb or chicken; however, consumers should verify halal certification at individual restaurants. 7. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule is not triggered. 8. All -1 sentinel values are documented in this notes field per the g3 sentinel values documentation rule.
